title: Pâte de fruit — raspberry
subtitle: French raspberry fruit jellies set with pectin and coated in sugar
excerpt: Intensely flavoured, jewel-like fruit jellies made from cooked raspberry purée and pectin, set in a thin layer, cut into squares, and rolled in caster sugar. The texture sits between a firm jam and a gummy sweet — concentrated fruit, clean flavour. Made at home to a quality most sweets shops cannot match.
sourceNotes: Pâte de fruit is a traditional French confection, documented in French confectionery manuals from at least the eighteenth century. The technique of cooking fruit purée with sugar, pectin, and glucose to a precise temperature and then setting it in a mould appears in French pastry and confectionery literature including Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de cuisine (1867), and various later French confectionery manuals. The raspberry version is one of the most classic types. The recipe here follows the established formula for pectin-set fruit jellies.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a cool, dry place for up to three weeks. Do not refrigerate — condensation causes the sugar coating to dissolve.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required. The mixture is cooked on the hob to 107°C — the gel point for a pectin-set confection. A sugar thermometer is essential; without one, the setting point cannot be reliably judged.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pâte de fruit must reach 107°C before being poured into the mould. At this temperature the pectin and sugar form a stable gel that sets firm enough to cut and coat in sugar without dissolving. Under-cooking produces a soft, sticky result that will not hold its shape; over-cooking produces a hard, almost solid block that is difficult to cut. A sugar thermometer is essential — the gel point cannot be reliably judged by appearance alone, since the mixture looks thick and glossy from 103°C onwards.

content.text: Pâte de fruit is one of the more exacting confections in the home kitchen because everything depends on hitting the

precisely. The hot mixture burns badly on contact with skin — if it splashes, run cold water over the area for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Use a high-sided saucepan and long-handled tools.

Line a 20 x 20 cm tin or baking tray with baking paper. Purée the raspberries and pass through a fine-mesh sieve to remove all seeds — you need about 350 ml of strained purée. Pour into a large, heavy saucepan. Mix 50 g of the caster sugar with the pectin powder and stir into the cold purée. Bring to the boil over medium-high heat, stirring constantly.

Add the remaining 450 g of caster sugar and the glucose syrup and stir to combine. Clip the sugar thermometer to the pan. Cook over medium-high heat, stirring constantly, until the mixture reaches 107°C — this takes 15 to 20 minutes and the mixture bubbles vigorously. If the mixture splashes onto skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Remove from the heat and stir in the lemon juice. Pour immediately into the lined tin and spread level. Leave to set at room temperature for at least 4 hours, or overnight. When fully set, the surface will be firm and not sticky. Turn out, peel off the paper, and cut into 2.5 cm squares with a sharp knife. Roll each square in caster sugar to coat all sides. Store in a single layer in an airtight tin.
